title: Set Your Mind on Christ
speaker: Elder Andrew Hoy
source_files: Set Your Mind on Christ - Elder Andrew Hoy - Colossians 3_1-17.txt

subpoints: A. The literary center of Paul's letter — doctrine and practice unified
B. Errors Paul addresses: paganism, syncretism, and legalism all diminish Christ's sufficiency
C. The implicit error of earthly-mindedness — not a call to ignore civic life

outline.point: II. Set Your Mind — The Call to Effort
subpoints: A. 'Set' is an active command — attending church, reading Scripture, fellowship are all entailed
B. Engaging in spiritual habits haphazardly without full engagement is insufficient
C. We are not alone — we have been raised with Christ and Christ intercedes for us at the Father's right hand

outline.point: III. Mind and Affections Are Inseparable
subpoints: A. King James reads 'set your affections' — both translations are correct together
B. Knowing in the biblical sense means intimate acquaintance that changes the whole person
C. The mind is the instrument through which affections are shaped (Colossians 3:10)

outline.point: IV. What We Are to Set Our Minds On
subpoints: A. Christ's person and work — his majesty and redemption in Colossians 1:13-18
B. Christ's ways — the practical exhortations to put off and put on in Colossians 3:5-14
C. Christ's church — the corporate pursuit of holiness, 'one body,' called together (Colossians 3:15-17)
A. Our hope rests in the church, the one institution God has promised to uphold
B. Political and earthly concerns belong below devotion to Christ and his church
C. Identify specific sins to repent of and specific ways to set the mind on Christ this week

summary:
Preaching from Colossians 3:1-17, Elder Andrew Hoy explores Paul's central exhortation to 'set your mind on things above' as the literary and theological hinge of the entire letter. He situates the text against the errors Paul was combating in Colossae — a blend of paganism, syncretism, and legalism — all of which share the common fault of drawing believers away from the sole and exclusive sufficiency of Christ. He also addresses the implicit error of earthly-mindedness, arguing that Paul is not calling for an other-worldly indifference to civic life, but rather for a Christ-centered orientation that makes true engagement with the world possible.

The sermon unfolds in three movements: the call to deliberate effort in setting the mind ('set' is an active imperative), the inseparability of mind and affections in biblical knowing, and the specific content that the mind is to dwell on — Christ's person and work, his ways, and his church. Elder Hoy emphasizes that this effort is not performed alone; believers have been raised with Christ and Christ himself intercedes for them at the Father's right hand. The sermon closes with a challenge to examine what has unduly consumed one's attention and to identify concrete steps toward setting the mind on Christ in the coming week.
transcript:
## Scripture Reading: Colossians 3:1-17

If you're able, please remain standing for the reading of the Word. We're reading Colossians chapter 3, verses 1 through 17 this morning.

If then you are raised with Christ, seek those things which are above, where Christ is, sitting at the right hand of God. Set your mind on things above, not on things on the earth. For you died, and your life is hidden with Christ in God. When Christ who is our life appears, then you also will appear with Him in glory. Therefore, put to death your members, which are on earth, fornication, uncleanness, passion, evil desire, and covetousness, which is idolatry. Because of these things, the wrath of God is coming upon the sons of disobedience, in which you yourselves once walked when you lived in them. But now, you yourselves are to put off all these: anger, wrath, malice, blasphemy, filthy language out of your mouth. Do not lie to one another, since you have put off the old man with his deeds, and have put on the new man who is renewed in knowledge according to the image of him who created him, where there is neither Greek nor Jew, circumcised nor uncircumcised, Barbarian, Scythian, slave or free, but Christ is all and in all. Therefore, as the elect of God, holy and beloved, put on tender mercies, kindness, humility, meekness, longsuffering, bearing with one another and forgiving one another. If anyone has a complaint against another, even as Christ forgave you, so you also must do. But above all these things put on love, which is the bond of perfection, and let the peace of God rule in your hearts, to which also you were called in one body; and be thankful. Let the word of Christ dwell in you richly in all wisdom, teaching and admonishing one another in psalms and hymns and spiritual songs, singing with grace in your hearts to the Lord. And whatever you do in word or deed, do it all in the name of the Lord Jesus, giving thanks to God the Father through Him.

This is the Word of the Lord. Let us pray together. Gracious God, we pray that your Spirit would be at work among us, that you would give us attentive minds and open hearts to receive the things that you have to give us this day in your Word. May we be blessed upon hearing and receive it in faith. In Christ's name we pray. Amen. Please be seated.

## Introduction

So when I think back to my school experience as a child, I remember every time I was given a writing assignment, agonizing over choosing a topic. I don't know if this is a common experience, but it's still a struggle that I have today. However, when I was considering what text I would preach from this morning, there was a passage from Paul that I have not been able to shake from my mind for the past few weeks. It was his exhortation to the church at Colossae to set your mind on things above.

These words from the third chapter of Colossians struck me a couple weeks ago — it was actually the week of all the tumultuous events taking place in Washington, D.C. And as I was going through my morning Bible reading, I was seriously convicted by these words and how much I had let my thoughts be caught up in the events of the day. So as I meditated on this, I felt the call to set my mind on Christ. And as I considered this experience and the way these words struck me on that day, I thought that I am certainly not alone in needing to receive this call.

There are so many things in this moment that are competing for our attention. We have everything around the election and the heated aftermath and the newly installed president. We have disagreements with government responses to the multitude of situations, whether it's COVID or homelessness or riots. There are some who fear COVID, there are some who fear collapsed economy. Then there are also the strained relationships that are emerging from the different opinions about all of these things. And even those of you who may not have been caught up in all this — maybe you just turn off the news and check out — there's still all the temptations we face every day, just the stresses and worries of the day, finances, friends, the future. There is so much that competes for our attention.

So this morning I invite us all to heed the call to set our minds on things above — that is, on Christ — to consider the riches that we have in him and the appropriate response to what we've been given.

This morning we will be spending our time considering the meaning of Paul's exhortation from Colossians to set our minds on things above. Specifically we will be looking at the context in which they're placed to help us understand what Paul actually means by these words. And as we do so I really believe that it will become clear that when Paul says to set your minds on things above, what he really means is that we need to be so consumed with Christ in our minds and our affections that we experience an inevitable overflow of holy living.

Kids, I know it can be difficult to focus in on the sermon Sunday mornings. If you get nothing else this morning I want you to know this: when we spend our time thinking about Jesus, we learn to love him and we learn to love his ways. And when we love Jesus, when we truly love him, when we learn to truly love his ways, we will do those things because we love him.

## I. The Context of Colossians 3:2

So let us begin by taking a moment to consider the broader context in which this verse takes place before we narrow our focus down to look at the exact words and what Paul actually meant by his exhortation to set your mind on things above.

This verse is in chapter three, verse two — right in the middle of the book of Colossians. And if it's not in the literal middle by word count, it is certainly the literary middle. The first half of the book is filled with doctrinal expositions in which Paul addresses some errors confronting the Colossian church. And if you move on to the second half — really chapters three and four — that's very practical in its orientation with exhortations to put off the old man and put on the new. What we see in the middle of these two parts is this verse, which is calling us to set our minds on things above, pulling these two halves of Paul's letter together.

Paul is never able to fully disentangle doctrinal explanations from practical exhortations. Even when the focus is mainly doctrinal, he's always jumping off into practical areas. Likewise, when his main focus is on the practical, he can't help but pointing back to those truths he's already articulated. Right in the middle stands this call to set our mind on things above.

We also see this in chapter three verse five, which begins "therefore put to death your members" — pointing back to what Paul has said about setting our minds on things above. And in verse twelve we see the same pattern: "therefore, as the elect of God, holy and beloved" — pointing back.

Paul also addressed specific errors that have emerged in the Colossian church, specifically those which challenge the sufficiency of Christ. Scholars aren't in full agreement on what this specific error is; it's not quite the Gnosticism John was addressing in 1 John or the Judaizing error Paul addressed in Galatians. Rather it's a blend: some paganism with the worship of angels (2:18), some syncretism — a Christ-plus-whatever-other-god-you-want-to-serve mentality (1:19-20), and some legalism in the ceremonies Paul confronts (2:11-21). Each of these errors has a common factor: they draw us away from the sole and exclusive sufficiency of Christ.

There is also an implicit error worth addressing: earthly-mindedness. This mindset says, "Give me a break — 'set your mind on things above'? We have pressing matters in this world!" This view falsely sets heavenly-mindedness against the concerns of the world. Paul is not telling us to be so heavenly-minded that we're of no earthly good. Within the very letter, Paul gives directions to the family — the basic building block of society — which demonstrates that Christian engagement in the world flows from setting our minds on Christ, not from ignoring him.

## II. Set Your Mind — The Call to Effort

So now let's consider how the rest of the book helps us understand what Paul means by "set your minds on things above." What I hear Paul saying is this: that we are to be so consumed in our minds and affections with Christ — in his person, his ways, and his church — that our lives naturally begin to look more like his.

Let's begin with the simple three words: set your mind. We see in these words that we are to put effort into our intellectual and mental life. Beginning with the single word "set" — this is a call to action on your part. You are the active agent. You must act.

What does setting your mind entail? Going to church and receiving the proclamation of God's Word. Reading and studying the Bible. Surrounding yourself with people who will speak the truth of God into your life. You have to put yourself in a context in which you will be drawn to the things above, the things of Christ. It will not happen on its own.

But don't think that just doing those things is sufficient. It's so easy to slip into the routine — yes, Bible every morning and church every week — and yet engage in these activities in a haphazard way, without fully engaging your mind and heart. Have you found that you attend church week in and week out and can't recall a single thing you heard from the pulpit for months? How about your Bible reading — you do it in the morning and not a single word comes back to mind throughout the rest of the day?

Take Paul's call to action this morning and take the effort needed to actually make this happen. But also know that this is not something you do alone. Look at the spiritual realities Paul addresses in 3:1. Before he even exhorts us to set our minds, he reminds us that we have been raised with Christ. You have been renewed, regenerated. You have the power of the Holy Spirit. And not only that, but Christ is sitting at the right hand of the Father interceding on your behalf. Jesus is praying for you right now. So if when you hear this exhortation you feel like you can't — that's a lie. For two reasons: you have been raised with Christ, and even more importantly, Jesus is praying for you.

## III. Mind and Affections Are Inseparable

What does Paul mean by "mind"? If you have a King James version this morning, you might be confused because it reads "set your affections." So which is it — the mind or the affections? It's both.

The action of the mind in the Bible is always more than mere comprehension. In the Bible, knowing something is always about intimate acquaintance — to be intimate with the things that you know. As Pastor Tito once said, if you truly know the truth of Christianity, you're a saint. And knowing in the biblical sense is so much more than the way we use it on a daily basis. We use it just as being able to recall something. But in the Bible, to know something is to be so deeply acquainted with it and to embrace it as truth that it actually changes your life, your mind, and your affections. So mind and affections are not two things to be separated.

I think the translators of our modern versions have used "mind" helpfully because it is through our minds that our affections are shaped. You don't have the ability to reach inside yourself and turn down the affections dial. But the way that we calibrate our affections is through the mind — through the things that we choose to dwell on. This is explicitly stated in chapter 3:10, where Paul says you have put on the new man who is renewed in knowledge. It is through the mind that God transforms the heart and the affections.

## IV. What We Are to Set Our Minds On

So what are we to set our minds on? Paul doesn't leave us to speculate. He demonstrates at least three specific things: Christ's person and work, his ways, and his church.

### Christ's Person and Work

Verse 1 makes it explicit: seek those things which are above where Christ is seated at the right hand of the Father. We are to consider the realities which this represents — specifically who Christ is as revealed in Scripture. Paul pointed back to the majestic teaching in chapter 1:13-18, perhaps some of the clearest Christology in the New Testament. The one through whom all things were created, who upholds all things, who is sovereign, is the same one who gave his life to redeem his people. If you're looking for a practical way to practice setting your mind on things above this week, you'd be hard pressed to find a better passage to meditate upon and memorize than Colossians 1:13-18.

### Christ's Ways

If we believe truly that Christ is all-powerful, always good, and all-wise, the same must be true of his ways. In chapter 3:5-14 we see a host of practical exhortations. In verses 5-11, Paul addresses the things we need to put off — those things which are not of Christ. In verses 12-14, a list of things to put on — those things which characterize Christ. Both sections flow from verse 10: put on the new man according to the image of Christ. They are not an arbitrary list of dos and don'ts but the very character of Christ which he calls us to reflect.

### Christ's Church

The third thing might not be as obvious. Paul jumps to the church in verses 15-17. Verses 12-17 are not just a call to individual righteousness but a call to devote ourselves to one another — to the body of Christ. In verse 15, Paul says: let the peace of God rule in your hearts, to which you were also called in one body. This calling is not just independent holiness but a corporate pursuit of God.

The church is the closest thing to God's kingdom on earth. God has never promised to uphold the United States as a leading world power through all of history. But he has promised to uphold one institution — his church. And that is the only place in which our ultimate hope should rest. Please don't hear this as a call to be dismissive or complacent toward civic engagement. There is a place for it. But that place is below our devotion to Christ and his church, and it is not until his kingdom is first in your mind that you can engage the things of this world in a God-honoring and fruitful way.

## V. Closing Application

In closing — my girls sat through this sermon once already this morning. They took notes and tallied how many times I said "set your mind on things above." One said ten, the other said twenty, and they figured it was probably more like thirty. I hope we have not missed that point this morning and have not walked away thinking this is some sort of sentimental positive thinking. There is serious substance to this call: Christ, his person, his work, his ways, and his church.

This call entails serious effort — but an effort rooted in the fact that we have been redeemed, enabled by our changed nature, and upheld by Christ's intercession on our behalf.

If I have one final exhortation, I'd ask you to consider specific ways this applies to you. Are there specific things you need to repent of today? Are there specific ways you can work to set your mind on the things of Christ? Don't let those things slip from mind.

Let us pray. Dear Father, we thank you for the mercy that you have shown us in Jesus Christ. That you have made us anew, that you've called us to set Christ above all else in our hearts and our minds and our affections. And that you have not left us to ourselves to accomplish this. But through Christ's intercession, we are constantly strengthened and renewed in this call. May your Spirit be at work in our hearts this day. In Christ's name we pray. Amen.
